item.title: Is Nvidia too big to fail?
item.description: ‘You’re clearly at the centre of everything’

item.title: How AI turbocharged Trump’s ‘slopaganda’ machine
item.description: US president’s stream of fake imagery on Truth Social is reshaping the boundaries of political communication

item.title: Global EV and hybrid sales to hit 30% of car market this year, IEA forecasts
item.description: Driver response to rising fuel prices and lower vehicle costs expected to propel sales

item.title: AI Exchange
item.description: In this monthly series, running alongside our existing Tech Exchange dialogues, FT journalists talk to the scientists, developers and business leaders exploring ever more applications for artificial intelligence, in every aspect of our lives

item.title: Introducing: The Story of Money
item.description: A new video podcast from The FT exploring the history of finance

item.title: To the moon: SpaceX’s generational payday
item.description: Who’s positioned for a windfall from the listing, and why it’s historic in more ways than one

item.title: Marc Rowan accused of misusing Apollo resources for political work
item.description: Chief executive of private capital group used company email and staff for lobbying, unions say

item.title: Merger spirits could lift the UK’s languishing mid-caps
item.description: S&P 400 index makes it well suited to active investing — and opportunistic dealmaking

item.title: UK housebuilders suffer £8bn hit from Iran war
item.description: Share prices have fallen sharply as hedge funds bet the industry will bear the brunt of the economic fallout

item.title: Big Europe and Asian private equity health funds merge to defy AI disruption
item.description: Global Healthcare Opportunities and CBC Group say $21bn investment manager will be world’s largest in sector

item.title: The Gulf crisis may just be starting
item.description: Oil futures markets are sanguine, but history shows expectations have often been disappointed

item.title: A guide to ‘greedflation’
item.description: Prices are never more dangerous than when they are subdued

item.title: NHS to trial GPs no longer handing out ‘sick notes’
item.description: Doctors to test four pilot schemes as ministers seek to reduce the 2.8mn signed off with long-term sickness

item.title: Protests spread in Africa as fuel crisis deepens
item.description: Sharp rises in diesel and petrol prices spark violent clashes in Kenya and Comoros as well as strikes in Mozambique

item.title: Hg spins out €500mn of assets from €19bn software group Visma
item.description: Move by private equity owner comes as long-awaited London IPO of group is shelved

item.title: The impossible maths of the AI boom
item.description: The IPO of big sector companies is probably nothing more than a transfer of investment risk to retail investors

item.title: Nato allies press US for clarity on troop cuts in Europe
item.description: Washington must co-ordinate with European capitals to avoid Nato deterrence gap against Russia, says Estonian minister

item.title: The Russian-born entrepreneur shaking up European rearmament
item.description: Mikhail Kokorich’s Destinus has risen rapidly to challenge incumbent defence groups with its cheaper missile systems

item.title: Britons use overseas clinics to pick babies’ sex
item.description: Fertility clinics say a growing number of people are travelling abroad for embryo screening procedures that are banned in the UK

item.title: Monaco is trying to shed its shady tax-haven image
item.description: The principality has become younger, safer, richer — and far less boring

item.title: Nvidia’s Huang bankrolls AI boom with $90bn deal spree
item.description: Chipmaker’s spending rivals Big Tech’s biggest venture operations, tying customers and start-ups to its technology

item.title: Harvey's Winston Weinberg: Why AI will force lawyers to change their fee structure
item.description: The co-founder of the legal start-up talks about how AI could shake up law firms’ business models and how he plans to stay ahead of rivals

item.title: Private equity’s new escape hatch keeps unsold companies in limbo
item.description: Growing use of so-called CV squared funds highlights downbeat era for public offerings to realise gains

item.title: The deal that put the dollar at the centre of the world
item.description: The Bretton Woods agreement: a tale of boozing, womanising, espionage and a heart attack

item.title: AI Labs: Sam Altman may make or break OpenAI
item.description: Will OpenAI’s chief be its greatest asset or its greatest liability?

item.title: Morgan Stanley issues China-only iPhones to its Hong Kong bankers
item.description: US bank’s move reflects rising concern over data security for staff travelling to mainland China

item.title: The stock market that outpaced Nasdaq’s dotcom-era gains
item.description: South Korea’s Kospi triples in 18 months powered by Samsung and SK Hynix as AI euphoria continues

item.title: Power from Sizewell C will be more expensive than Hinkley Point, says UK watchdog
item.description: National Audit Office report says consumers will pay higher amount for energy from Suffolk project compared to its Somerset counterpart

item.title: FirstFT: Trump claims Xi promised not to send weapons to Tehran
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Google unveils new version of AI model and Japan and South Korea agree to deepen energy co-operation

item.title: Arsenal secure first English Premier League title in 22 years
item.description: Mikel Arteta’s side take football crown after years of near misses as rivals Manchester City drop points
